title: Python packaging
body:
## What this PR does

This PR adds a complete Python packaging story to the project:

1. **A self-contained installable wheel** — `pip install` / `uv sync` builds and
   installs the `mlir_ktdp` Python bindings + all native libraries. No separate
   MLIR installation required at runtime.

2. **A pure-Python utilities package** (`tools_ktdp`) — ships alongside
   `mlir_ktdp` in the wheel. Fully live-editable: changes to Python files take
   effect immediately without rebuilding.

3. **A `ktdp-walk` CLI tool** — inspect any `.mlir` file's operation tree from
   the command line.

4. **A pytest suite** — three parametrized fixtures covering dialect
   registration, flat KTDP ops, and nested control flow.

---

## How it installs

The build backend is [scikit-build-core](https://scikit-build-core.readthedocs.io/),
which drives cmake under the hood. `uv` manages the virtual environment and
locks all dependencies.

```
uv sync --extra mlir
```

That single command:
- Creates/updates the venv
- Installs `mlir_wheel` (provides MLIR cmake config + Python support libs)
- Runs cmake → builds the C++ extension + CAPI library
- Installs the project in **editable** mode

---

## Frozen dependency versions (`uv.lock`)

All versions are pinned in `uv.lock` for reproducible installs:

| Package | Pinned version | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `mlir-wheel` | `20260409+3b3ac5a11` | MLIR cmake config + bundled Python support libs |
| `nanobind` | `2.12.0` | Python ↔ C++ binding library (runtime dep) |
| `pytest` | `9.0.3` | Test runner (`--extra test`) |
| `lit` | `18.1.8` | LIT test runner (`--extra test`) |

`mlir_wheel` is an **optional** dep — users with a custom MLIR build can pass
`CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=..."` and skip it entirely.

---

## Wheel contents

The wheel is self-contained (~19 MB compressed, ~70 MB uncompressed). No
`mlir_wheel` needed at runtime.

```
ktir_mlir_frontend-0.1.0-cp312-cp312-linux_aarch64.whl
│
├── mlir_ktdp/                         ← cmake-managed
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── ir.py, passmanager.py, rewrite.py
│   ├── dialects/
│   │   ├── ktdp_nanobind.py           ← KTDP dialect Python bindings
│   │   ├── builtin.py, ext.py
│   │   └── _ktdp_ops_gen.py, _ods_common.py, ...
│   ├── extras/
│   │   └── meta.py, types.py
│   └── _mlir_libs/
│       ├── libKtdpPythonCAPI.so       34 MB  ← statically links all MLIR C API
│       ├── libMLIRPythonSupport.so     1.5 MB
│       ├── _mlir.cpython-312-*.so      0.3 MB ← core MLIR Python extension
│       ├── _ktdpDialectsNanobind-*.so  0.1 MB ← KTDP nanobind extension
│       └── libnanobind-mlir_ktdp.so   0.2 MB
│
└── tools_ktdp/                        ← scikit-build-core managed (pure Python)
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py                    ← entry point for ktdp-walk
    └── ir_utils.py                   ← ktdp_context(), walk_module()
```

`libKtdpPythonCAPI.so` dominates size — it statically links all MLIR dialect
C API libraries so no other MLIR installation is needed at runtime.

---

## ktdp-walk demo

Parse any `.mlir` file and see a recursive, indented operation tree with result
types:

```
$ uv run ktdp-walk test/Ktdp/add-with-control-flow.mlir

builtin.module: []
  func.func: []
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    ktdp.get_compute_tile_id: [index]
    arith.muli: [index]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    scf.for: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      tensor.empty: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      linalg.add: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
        arith.addf: [f16]
        linalg.yield: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.store: []
      scf.yield: []
    func.return: []
```

Indentation reflects nesting depth — `scf.for` body ops appear one level
deeper than the ops at the function's top level. Also usable as a library:

```python
from tools_ktdp import ktdp_context
from tools_ktdp.ir_utils import walk_module

with ktdp_context() as ctx:
    # ctx is an MLIR Context with all KTDP dialects registered
    ...

for op, depth in walk_module(source):
    print(f"{'  ' * depth}{op.name}")
```

---

## Tests

Four tests in `python/test/test_basic.py`, all passing:

```
$ uv run pytest python/test/ -v

platform linux -- Python 3.12.13, pytest-9.0.3
collected 4 items

test_basic.py::test_dialect_registration                PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[SIMPLE_MODULE]                 PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_BASIC_MODULE]             PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE]      PASSED

4 passed in 0.02s
```

Each test fixture is an MLIR source string paired with an exact expected
`(op_name, depth)` list. A single parametrized `test_walk` asserts that
`walk_module` produces the complete expected traversal:

| Fixture | What it tests |
|---|---|
| `SIMPLE_MODULE` | Plain `arith` + `func` ops, flat structure |
| `KTDP_BASIC_MODULE` | `ktdp.construct_memory_view`, `ktdp.construct_access_tile`, `ktdp.load`, `ktdp.store` — all at depth 2 |
| `KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE` | `scf.for` loop containing KTDP ops — verifies that `ktdp.load` and `ktdp.construct_access_tile` appear at depth 3, deeper than `scf.for` at depth 2 |

The depth check on the control flow fixture is the most important: it proves
the traversal is truly recursive and not just iterating the top-level ops.

---

## Developer workflows

### Recommended: `uv sync` with mlir_wheel

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --extra mlir --extra test   # build + install + test deps
uv run pytest python/test/          # run Python tests
uv run ktdp-walk path/to/file.mlir  # use the CLI tool
```

Edits to `tools_ktdp/` are live immediately. Edits to `mlir_ktdp/` or C++
require `uv sync` to rebuild.

### Custom MLIR build (no mlir_wheel)

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --no-install-project --extra test
CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir" uv pip install .
```

### Bare cmake — C++ only (no Python)

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
```

Python bindings are **off by default** — the cmake option
`KTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS` (default `OFF`) guards the entire Python setup
block. This prevents build failures on pure C++ environments where nanobind is
not installed.

### Bare cmake — with Python bindings

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja \
    -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir \
    -DKTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS=ON \
    -DPython3_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) \
    -DLLVM_EXTERNAL_LIT=$(which lit)
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
# then:
PYTHONPATH=build/python_packages/ktdp python ...
```

### LIT tests

```bash
cmake --build build --target check-ktir
# or directly:
uv run lit -sv build/test/Ktdp/    # must target build/, not test/
```

---

## Package architecture: why two packages?

`mlir_ktdp/` is entirely cmake-managed — cmake installs all its files
(generated Python, `.so` extensions, CAPI library) as part of the build.
Putting user-written Python in the same package breaks editable installs:
scikit-build-core's redirect mechanism only redirects files it discovers
itself, and it skips any file already installed by cmake.

`tools_ktdp/` is pure Python with no cmake involvement. scikit-build-core
manages it directly via `wheel.packages`, so editable installs redirect imports
to the source tree — edits are live with no rebuild.

title: Add VectorBT backtesting ADR and YAML strategy loader
body:
## Summary
- Add ADR documenting the decision to migrate backtesting from ta4j (Java) to VectorBT (Python) at `specs/vectorbt-backtesting/SPEC.md`
- Implement `YamlStrategyLoader` that reads YAML strategy specs and generates entry/exit signals for VectorBT backtesting
- Add integration tests proving end-to-end: YAML strategy spec → signal generation → VectorBT backtest → performance metrics

## Test plan
- [ ] `bazel test //services/backtesting:test_yaml_strategy_loader` passes
- [ ] `bazel test //services/backtesting:test_vectorbt_runner` still passes (existing tests unaffected)
- [ ] Review ADR at `specs/vectorbt-backtesting/SPEC.md` for completeness

Closes #1625

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Feature/c to python
body:
This pull request introduces support for Python bindings in ACES, adds a new C API for meteorology registry queries, and improves build system organization and documentation. The changes enable building and testing Python bindings via CMake, document the Python API, and add a C header for Fortran/C interoperability. Additionally, the CMake configuration is refactored for clarity, and new source files are included for configuration validation and the C API.

**Python Bindings Integration**
- Added a `BUILD_PYTHON_BINDINGS` CMake option to enable building Python bindings for ACES, with corresponding logic to add the `src/python` subdirectory and tests when enabled. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aR46-R48) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aR323-R412)
- Expanded documentation to describe the Python API, including function/class tables and exception hierarchy, and linked the Python bindings guide from the main documentation. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-9eddf4dc51bf6a0125ca7fb094468ad284112270385c41cebce4f8f0a29620abR34-R81) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-b4d68dc855d0f9476d3f2ee343853bd21bf82ea9960d0cf06661baa244439dd6R12) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-b4d68dc855d0f9476d3f2ee343853bd21bf82ea9960d0cf06661baa244439dd6L31-R32)

**C API for Meteorology Registry**
- Added a new header `aces_c_api.h` providing C-linkage functions for querying meteorology variable names and aliases, facilitating Fortran NUOPC cap interoperability.
- Extended the `AcesConfig` struct to include a `met_registry` mapping internal meteorology variable names to their aliases.

**Build System and Test Improvements**
- Refactored the `CMakeLists.txt` for clarity: cleaned up comments, improved formatting, and consolidated test and source file listings. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL84-L108) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL178-R170) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL209-L218) [[4]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL230) [[5]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL248-L287) [[6]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL312-L347) [[7]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL358-L414)
- Added Python-based tests for the bindings, ensuring pytest is installed and using CTest fixtures for reliable test execution.

**Code Quality Targets**
- Improved clang-format and clang-tidy targets by using more descriptive variable names and cleaning up file globbing for source file discovery.

**Fortran and Dependency Handling**
- Minor adjustments to Fortran build logic and dependency discovery to improve reliability and maintainability. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL15-L22) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-1e7de1ae2d059d21e1dd75d5812d5a34b0222cef273b7c3a2af62eb747f9d20aL117-R104)

These changes collectively enhance ACES' extensibility, Python interoperability, and developer experience.

##### Enhancements

- Fix `build_system.requires` error message ([#​18911](https://redirect.github.com/astral-sh/uv/pull/18911))
- Remove trailing path separators in path normalization ([#​18915](https://redirect.github.com/astral-sh/uv/pull/18915))

title: Fix vulnerable dependency versions: python-jose and python-multipart
body:
Two pip dependencies had known CVEs requiring version bumps in both `requirements.txt` and `pyproject.toml`.

## Changes

- **`python-jose`** `3.3.0` → `3.4.0`: algorithm confusion vulnerability with OpenSSH ECDSA keys
- **`python-multipart`** `0.0.9` → `0.0.22`: arbitrary file write (non-default config) and DoS via malformed `multipart/form-data` boundary

title: [codex] upgrade python packages
body:
## What changed
- refreshed the `uv` lockfile to upgrade the resolved Python dependency set
- updated `rich` from `14.3.3` to `14.3.4`
- left `pyproject.toml` unchanged because the project already uses unconstrained direct dependency specs

## Why
- bring the repo onto the latest compatible package set available through the existing `uv` workflow
- keep dependency maintenance isolated to a lockfile-only refresh

## Impact
- no application code changes
- runtime and dev environments now resolve to the updated `rich` release
- the only remaining outdated transitive reported by `uv tree --outdated` was `pydantic-core`, which is constrained by the currently resolved `pydantic` from `xdk`

## Validation
- `uv run ruff check`
- `uv run pytest`

Both checks passed.

title: fix: eliminate protoc/Java deprecation warnings and add CI regression gate
body:
## Summary

- **Proto**: Remove unused `rpc.proto` import from `diff.proto`; add build.sbt post-process step to replace deprecated `.PARSER` field with `.parser()` method in protoc-generated code
- **Java API**: Replace deprecated `JsonParser()`, `Class.newInstance()`, `PosixParser`, `OptionBuilder`, `StringEscapeUtils` with modern equivalents; add `@SafeVarargs` for unchecked varargs
- **Generated code**: Fix PST template (`enumHelpers.st`) to use `forNumber()` instead of deprecated `valueOf(int)` for protobuf enum translation
- **CI gate**: Add `-Xlint:deprecation` and `-Xlint:unchecked` to `javacOptions` in both root and PST subprojects to surface all warnings going forward

## Changes by category

| Category | Files | Fix |
|----------|-------|-----|
| Proto unused import | `diff.proto` | Remove unused import |
| Protobuf PARSER deprecation | `build.sbt` (post-process) | `.PARSER` → `.parser()` |
| Protobuf valueOf deprecation | `enumHelpers.st` | `valueOf()` → `forNumber()` |
| Gson JsonParser deprecation | `WebSocketChannel`, `RobotSerializer`, `GsonUtil` | Static `parseString()` |
| Class.newInstance() | `ProtoSerializer` | `getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance()` |
| Commons CLI | `AbstractCliRobotAgent`, `PstCommandLine` | `DefaultParser`, `Option.builder()` |
| Commons Lang3 | `FolderServlet` | → `commons-text` `StringEscapeUtils` |
| Unchecked varargs | `ApiDocsServlet` | `@SafeVarargs` on `list()` |
| Intentional deprecation | `AbstractLogger`, `WaveletBasedConversation` | `@SuppressWarnings` |

## Test plan

- [x] `sbt clean compile` — zero deprecation/unchecked warnings
- [x] `sbt compileGwt` — successful GWT compilation
- [ ] CI build passes (GitHub Actions)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Implement include_vars support and Level 18 variable precedence
body:
Implemented `include_vars` action plugin support and established Level 18 variable precedence in the execution engine. This involved updates to both the Java core (`VariableManager`, `TaskQueueManager`) and the Python-to-Java bridge (`ansible_bridge.py`, `ansible_action_launcher.py`) to handle runtime variable loading from external files and directories. A new integration test verifies the functionality and precedence levels.

---
*PR created automatically by Jules for task [8615462549745642894](https://jules.google.com/task/8615462549745642894) started by @yamanakahirofumi*

title: Refactor AnsibleModule to Java implementation
body:
This PR moves the `AnsibleModule` implementation from Python (`ansible_bridge.py`) to Java (`PythonAnsibleModuleMock.java`). This refactoring improves the maintainability and performance of the module mock by utilizing Java's native capabilities for file system operations and hashing, while maintaining the necessary Python interface for Ansible module compatibility. Key changes include:
- A new `PythonAnsibleModuleMock` class in Java that implements parameter specification parsing, `run_command` delegation, and various hashing and file attribute methods.
- Injection of a `Factory` for this mock into the GraalPy context via `TaskExecutor`.
- Delegation of `AnsibleModule` logic in `ansible_bridge.py` to the Java-backed mock.
- Improved stability by using `_deep_convert` to normalize complex types before they cross the Java-Python boundary.

---
*PR created automatically by Jules for task [3831848965427245340](https://jules.google.com/task/3831848965427245340) started by @yamanakahirofumi*

title: Refactor AnsibleModule bridge logic
body:
Refactored the `AnsibleModule` bridge logic by migrating state management and decision-making from the Python layer to the Java layer. This simplifies the Python implementation and leverages Java for core processing. Key changes include:
- Moving flag initialization and storage to Java.
- Implementing result preparation logic (`getExitArgs`, `getFailArgs`) in Java.
- Delegating `boolean` evaluation to Java's `Truthiness` utility.
- Reducing state maintenance in the Python script by managing file attributes in Java.

---
*PR created automatically by Jules for task [1676815445474160715](https://jules.google.com/task/1676815445474160715) started by @yamanakahirofumi*

title: Python PyPI distribution
body:
## Summary

- Wheel bundles cross-compiled native libs (.dylib for macOS, .so for Linux) — `pip install zerodev-aa` just works
- CI workflow cross-compiles 4 targets (macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux x86_64/arm64) and publishes to PyPI
- Auto-incrementing versions from `VERSION` file with `python-v*` tag namespace
- Verified end-to-end on Sepolia: context → account → sendUserOp → receipt

### Pre-requisite before first publish
- Add GitHub Secret: `PYPI_TOKEN` (API token from [pypi.org](https://pypi.org))
